content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Anchor Prompting: Reducing Hallucinations via Real-Time External Verification Prompts",
  "problem": "Current large language models often hallucinate facts in their responses, undermining trustworthiness. Existing prompting strategies insufficiently constrain generation to verifiable information.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes applications, factual reliability is critical. Despite retrieval-augmented methods, pure prompting approaches that minimize hallucinations remain underexplored. A new prompting design that actively encourages fact-grounded reasoning could enable safer, more trustworthy LLM outputs.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Anchor Prompting, which interleaves standard user prompts with dynamically generated subprompts that instruct the LLM to explicitly verify factual claims using up-to-date external sources via in-context retrieval snippets. The prompt format: (1) decomposes user questions into atomic fact queries, (2) injects retrieved evidence with provenance, and (3) constrains generation to only synthesize responses citing this evidence. The method is designed to work without architectural or finetuning changes, instead leveraging prompt engineering and retrieval augmentation tightly integrated within the input sequence.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate on factual QA datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, HotpotQA, OpenFact), comparing to vanilla prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented generation. Metrics include factual accuracy, hallucination rate, citation precision, and human evaluation. Ablations test the impact of decomposition, retrieval, and citation constraints."
}
